comments.body: It is not clear what you are trying to do. Add an explanation to the question of why you need to access the internals and be sure to add a minimal reproducible example.
I have added a piece of code I am using. Way more critical is the usage of 'libvlc_instance_t', though. I am subclassing 'MediaPlayer' which requires 'libvlc_instance_t' in the constructor.

I think I found the problem: packages inside 'uk.co.caprica.vlcj.natives' cannot be exposed in 'vlcj-4.10.1'. This was possible in 'vlcj-4.8.2'. When opening the Java Build Path in Eclipse, 'uk.co.caprica.vlcj.natives' no longer shows up in the "All Modules" section under the "Module Dependencies" tab. This is crucial to my project. I can stay on 'vlcj-4.8.2' for now, but it's definitely becoming a problem when moving to 'vlcj-5' once 'VLC 4.0' is being released. Added a screenshot for reference.
Would you want me to post a VLCJ GitHub issue for this? It would really be helpful if these changes could be reverted. Thanks!
How would I handle this problem in future releases of vlcj, then?

Alright, I have subscribed to the GitHub issue so I can track the progress. Just as a remark: I am sub-classing 'MediaPlayerFactory' as well, as it creates the instances of my MediaPlayer. The factory needs the 'libvlcInstance', too. The reason why I am doing all this is I have an object that needs to be passed to the MediaPlayer, and I need to read this object from the player in the event listener, e.g. when 'playing(mediaPlayer)' is being called.

answers.body:
Recent releases of vlcj moved to using the Java Module System and, as part of this, access to what should have been internal classes used to create bindings to the native library was restricted via module rules.

However, certain of those classes were needed for niche cases, like here sub-classing the media player and factory components.

As an aside, for most use cases of sub-classing, which tend to be requirements like associating custom data with each media player, there is a pre-existing "user data" API which could be used instead.

In any event, in a new release of vlcj, a public API wrapper for the private internal libvlc_instance_t class has been provided and this can be used to create sub-classes without encountering module errors.

This was introduced with vlcj 4.11.0.

title: Problem accessing LibX11 and libvlc_instance_t in vlcj-4.10.1
body:
I upgraded from vlcj-4.8.2 to vlcj-4.10.1 and recognized uk.co.caprica.vlcj.binding.lib.LibX11 and uk.co.caprica.vlcj.binding.internal.libvlc_instance_t are no longer accessible. I have been playing around with the module path and stuff, but somehow, I am not able to expose the respective module/classes. I am not very familiar with the Java module system and how it works tbh.

I am using Eclipse 2024-12. Does anybody know how to fix this?

A simple code example I am using is:

if (Platform.isUnix()) {
    LibX11.INSTANCE.XInitThreads();
}

Edit: Here's a screenshot of the Module Dependencies (vlcj-4.8.2)
